teaching-web-development-china-yango-uni.title: Teaching Web Development in China @ Yango University
teaching-web-development-china-yango-uni.excerpt: How teaching HTML, CSS, and JavaScript fundamentals in Fuzhou, China taught me to be a better developer. Lessons on cross-cultural education, communication, and why strong fundamentals matter more than frameworks.

how-i-built-orderbridge.title: How I Built OrderBridge: Solving the Delivery-to-POS Middleware Gap
how-i-built-orderbridge.excerpt: Building an OAuth 2.0 middleware system that automatically bridges DoorDash, Uber Eats, and delivery platforms to restaurant POS systems. Technical deep-dive into webhook ingestion, schema translation, and real-time order sync.

yangoArticle.lede: Shortly after the end of the Covid Era, I spent time teaching web development at Yango University in the city of Fuzhou, Fujian. I had always wanted to try life and work in China.

curriculum.label: [ THE CURRICULUM ]
curriculum.heading: Teaching the Foundations of the Web
curriculum.p1: My role was to introduce students to the fundamentals of web development.
topics: The client–server model behind web applications
curriculum.p3: Instead of only teaching theory, I tried to keep things practical as much as possible, classes needed to be fun, that is what motivates students the most. The goal was always to get students building something they could see working in the browser.
curriculum.p4: Even a simple landing page or interactive UI was enough to get people excited about programming.

yangoArticle.figStudentPresenting: Student presenting their project to the class

challenge.label: [ THE CHALLENGE ]
challenge.heading: Teaching in a Different Culture
challenge.p1: Teaching in China came with some unique challenges.
challenge.p2: Language differences meant that long technical explanations didn't always work well, I taught my classes in English, and not every student could understand. So I relied heavily on visual demonstrations, live coding, lots of diagrams, and examples.
challenge.p3: One thing I noticed quickly is that code itself becomes the common language. Once students start experimenting and seeing results in the browser, the barrier disappears.

insight.label: [ THE INSIGHT ]
insight.heading: Why Teaching Makes You a Better Developer
insight.p1: Teaching programming forces you to understand things at a deeper level.
insight.p2: When you have to explain concepts like:
concepts: Why CSS layouts behave the way they do
insight.p3: You quickly discover which parts you truly understand and which parts you don't.
insight.p4: That process made me a better developer in several ways:
insight.p5: All of those skills matter just as much as writing code.

perspective.label: [ PERSPECTIVE ]
perspective.heading: A Global Industry
perspective.p1: One thing that stood out to me while teaching in China was how global software development really is.
perspective.p2: Students in Fuzhou were learning the same core technologies used by developers everywhere: web standards, JavaScript, APIs, and modern development tools.
perspective.p3: The problems developers work on are surprisingly similar no matter where you are in the world.
perspective.p4: But yeah, infrastructure is quite different and confusing.

reflection.label: [ REFLECTION ]
reflection.heading: Looking Back
reflection.p1: Teaching web development was an experience that stayed with me. It reinforced something that I still believe strongly: strong fundamentals and clear thinking matter more than any particular framework or tool.
reflection.p2: And those fundamentals are the same whether you are teaching in a classroom or building real software.
reflection.calloutText: Strong fundamentals and clear thinking matter more than any particular framework or tool.

cta.label: [ MORE ARTICLES ]
cta.heading: Keep Reading

orderbridgeArticle.titleMain: How I Built OrderBridge: Solving the
orderbridgeArticle.lede: A few months ago, a client came to me with a problem that was simple to describe and it seemed quite straightforward. They needed to bridge the gap between major delivery platforms and their own POS system with an automated OAuth 2.0 handshake.
orderbridgeArticle.introP1Pre: This project started as a real client brief. The request came in Spanish:
orderbridgeArticle.introP1Post: In short: they needed to connect their clients' delivery accounts to their POS system through StreamOrder's partner API  -  automatically, via OAuth, with no manual intervention. I analysed the requirements, read through the StreamOrder partner documentation, and got to work.

brief.label: [ THE BRIEF ]
brief.heading: The Client Proposal
brief.p1: Before writing a single line of code, I put together a formal proposal. It forced clarity on both sides  -  the client understood exactly what they were getting, and I understood exactly what I was building.
brief.descriptionText: This project establishes a secure, automated "Digital Bridge"  -  a Middleware API between the StreamOrder aggregator and the client's Point of Sale (POS) system, enabling delivery orders to flow automatically into the POS with no manual intervention.
brief.objectiveText: Implement OAuth 2.0 as the authentication layer, ensuring data remains secure while allowing orders to sync automatically from delivery platforms into the POS.

objectives.label: Secure Connection
objectives.desc: OAuth handshake  -  no shared passwords, data protected by encrypted digital keys.

objectives.label: Automatic Sync
objectives.desc: Every DoorDash and delivery platform order appears in the POS instantly.

weeks.title: Security & The Digital Handshake
weeks.result: StreamOrder officially linked; data protected by encrypted OAuth keys.

weeks.title: The Automation Bridge
weeks.result: Translator and webhook listener live in a test environment  -  orders captured and translated instantly.

weeks.title: Dashboard & Live Launch
weeks.result: Bridge active, orders printing in the POS, live monitoring dashboard delivered.

weeks.title: Refinement & Monitoring
weeks.result: 7-day live monitoring window; API changes from StreamOrder or delivery platforms patched immediately.

deliverables.name: OAuth 2.0 Integration
deliverables.desc: Authentication layer connecting all accounts without sharing private passwords.

deliverables.name: Real-Time Order Injection
deliverables.desc: Orders detected via webhook and pushed directly into the POS within seconds.

deliverables.name: Automated Translator
deliverables.desc: Intelligent menu mapping ensuring every item, modifier, and customer note matches the POS format.

deliverables.name: Automatic Token Refresh
deliverables.desc: Self-maintaining security  -  OAuth tokens renew automatically so the connection never expires.

deliverables.name: Reliability Monitoring
deliverables.desc: Error-tracking that alerts immediately if an order fails to sync.

deliverables.name: Live Connection Dashboard
deliverables.desc: Web view to monitor bridge status and verify the most recent successful syncs.

prerequisites: StreamOrder Developer access with Partner API enabled.
POS developer portal credentials with write permissions.
Menu / SKU list exported from the POS (CSV or Excel).
A named technical point of contact authorised to approve the OAuth handshake.

painPoint.label: [ THE PAIN POINT ]
painPoint.heading: Keeping Track of All the Orders
painPoint.p1: Before we automated this, the workflow was boring and painful. Most busy kitchens have a counter full of tablets from different delivery apps.
listItems: Read the order off a tablet.
Manually type it into the POS.
Hope they didn't miss a "no onions" modifier or something.
painPoint.p2: In a quiet shop, it's boring. In a kitchen doing 80+ orders a day, it's a painful operational problem.
painPoint.mathPre: 50 orders a day × 2.5 minutes of manual entry × $18/hr =
painPoint.mathPost: to a task that shouldn't involve a human at all.

orderbridgeArticle.figOrders: Orders page  -  filterable, sortable, real-time status updates

engineering.label: [ ENGINEERING ]
engineering.heading: Engineering the Pipeline
engineering.p1: Once the ROI was clear, I mapped out the pipeline. The logic needed to be bulletproof:
engineering.figDiagram: High-level flow: delivery platforms → OrderBridge → restaurant POS

steps.title: Ingest delivery webhooks
steps.desc: Receive HTTPS POST from each delivery platform.

steps.title: Verify signatures
steps.desc: HMAC-SHA256 check  -  security before anything else.

steps.title: Translate schemas
steps.desc: Map disparate JSON payloads into a unified POS format.

steps.title: Inject the order
steps.desc: POST to the POS API with exponential-backoff retry.

steps.title: Broadcast updates
steps.desc: WebSocket push to the live dashboard  -  no polling.

process.label: [ THE PROCESS ]
process.heading: Writing the Spec First
process.p1: I started with a Software Requirements Specification (SRS) before writing a single line of code. It's much easier to fix a mistake in a document than to rewrite production code later. The SRS forced both me and the client to agree on exactly what "done" looked like  -  no ambiguity, no scope creep.

requirements.label: FR-01
requirements.title: OAuth 2.0 Server
requirements.desc: System must act as an OAuth 2.0 provider  -  issuing, validating, and refreshing tokens per RFC 6749.

requirements.label: FR-02
requirements.title: Webhook Ingestion
requirements.desc: System must accept HTTPS POST webhooks from delivery platforms and verify each request with HMAC-SHA256 signature validation.

requirements.label: FR-03
requirements.title: Schema Translation
requirements.desc: System must map all incoming delivery payloads to a unified POS format, with graceful failure and logging on unmapped SKUs.

requirements.label: FR-04
requirements.title: POS Injection
requirements.desc: System must POST translated orders to the POS API with exponential-backoff retry on failure  -  no order dropped silently.

requirements.label: NFR-01
requirements.title: Real-Time Updates
requirements.desc: Dashboard must reflect order status changes via WebSocket push. No polling.

requirements.label: NFR-02
requirements.title: Token Persistence
requirements.desc: OAuth tokens must survive server restarts, stored encrypted at rest.

orderbridgeArticle.figDashboard: Live dashboard  -  real-time order feed with status tracking

techStack.label: [ TECH STACK ]
techStack.heading: The Tech Stack
techStack.subtitle: What tools would I use for this kind of project:

items.name: Node.js & TypeScript
items.body: I am sure that Python would've also worked wonders here. Personally I prefer working with node modules and npm over pip and virtual environments.

items.name: Fastify (Not Express)
items.body: Express is the legacy default, but Fastify is built for modern performance. Built-in JSON schema validation, lower overhead. For a middleware API where every millisecond counts, Fastify wins.

deployment.label: [ DEPLOYMENT ]
deployment.heading: Infrastructure
deployment.p1: The three services are independently deployed  -  each with its own host, its own domain, and its own responsibility. This mirrors how a real production integration would be structured.

services.detail: Real-time monitoring UI  -  edge-deployed, globally available.
Core middleware engine with the OAuth server, webhook ingestion, schema translator, and POS injector. PostgreSQL stores OAuth tokens, menu mappings, and order logs.
Isolated POS simulator running as a completely separate service  -  its own OAuth consent flow, its own order terminal.

deployment.whySeparate: Keeping MockPOS on its own Railway service wasn't just convenient  -  it was the point. It proves the integration works across a real network boundary, not just between two functions in the same process.

hardParts.label: [ THE HARD PARTS ]
hardParts.heading: Translation and Trust
hardParts.p1: The Translator was the hardest part of this project. DoorDash, Uber Eats, and GrabFood all have wildly different ideas of how to describe a "Cheeseburger with no pickles." Mapping these inconsistent payloads to a standardised POS SKU while maintaining a MenuMapping table required deep edge-case handling. If the mapping fails, the system has to fail gracefully  -  not drop the order into a void.
hardParts.p2: Then there was the OAuth 2.0 Server. It's one thing to use OAuth; it's another to be the provider. Building the authorization endpoints, token logic, and encrypted storage according to the RFC specs took more gray hair than the rest of the app combined.

orderbridgeArticle.figMockPos: Phing Kai Kitchen [POS Simulator]  -  orders arriving automatically from OrderBridge

portfolio.label: [ THE PORTFOLIO BUILD ]
portfolio.heading: Introducing OrderBridge
portfolio.p1: I enjoyed the architecture of the client project so much that I built OrderBridge  -  a refined, standalone version for my portfolio. It's not just a copy; it's an evolution. A complete end-to-end demo that simulates the entire flow without needing real-world infrastructure.

repos.name: Dashboard
repos.body: Real-time monitoring and control UI for the OrderBridge system.

repos.name: POS Simulator (MockPOS)
repos.body: A secondary app representing a fictional restaurant called Phing Kai Kitchen. It acts as the 'target' POS, complete with its own OAuth consent screen and live order terminal.

orderbridgeArticle.figSimulator: Simulator  -  fire test orders from any delivery platform with one click

sourceCode.label: [ SOURCE CODE ]
sourceCode.heading: The Complete Codebase
sourceCode.subtitle: All three core components are open-source and available on GitHub:

repos.title: OrderBridge API
repos.desc: Core middleware engine with OAuth 2.0, webhook ingestion, and schema translation.

repos.title: OrderBridge Dashboard
repos.desc: Real-time monitoring UI with live order feed and connection status.

repos.title: MockPOS Server
repos.desc: POS simulator demonstrating the complete integration flow.

liveDemos.label: [ LIVE DEMOS ]
liveDemos.heading: Try It Out
liveDemos.subtitle: Both services are live and ready to use:

demos.name: OrderBridge Dashboard
demos.desc: Real-time monitoring interface. Watch orders flow through the pipeline as they arrive.

demos.name: MockPOS Server
demos.desc: Live orders page from the fictional Phing Kai Kitchen POS system. See orders injected in real time.

cta.label: [ SEE IT IN ACTION ]
cta.heading: Ready to Explore?
cta.subtitle: Dive into the source code, watch the live systems in action, or check out the full case study.

orderbridgeCaseStudy.tagline: A middleware system that connects food delivery platforms to restaurant POS systems  -  eliminating manual order entry entirely.
orderbridgeCaseStudy.diagramCaption: High-level flow: delivery platforms → OrderBridge → restaurant POS

problem.p1: Every time an order arrives from DoorDash, Uber Eats, or Grubhub, someone at the restaurant has to manually read it off a tablet and type it into their POS system. At 50 orders a day, that's over two hours of labour  -  every single day.
problem.p2: Manual entry introduces errors. A missed modifier, a wrong quantity, a skipped item. In a high-volume kitchen, one wrong order ripples into customer complaints, refunds, and wasted food.
problem.p3: The economics are straightforward: at 50 orders per day, 2.5 minutes of manual entry each, and $18/hr labour cost  -  that's over
problem.p3End: to a problem that should be automated.

solution.p1: OrderBridge sits between delivery platforms and the POS. When an order arrives, it receives the webhook, translates the payload into the POS format, and injects the order automatically  -  in under 800ms, with no human in the loop.

steps.title: Webhook Reception
steps.desc: Each delivery platform sends an HTTPS POST to a shared webhook endpoint. The server verifies the HMAC-SHA256 signature, rejects invalid payloads immediately, and deduplicates by order ID.

steps.title: Translation
steps.desc: A platform-specific handler maps the delivery schema to a standardised POS payload. Each item is resolved against a MenuMapping table that links platform item IDs to POS SKUs.

steps.title: Injection
steps.desc: The translated payload is POSTed to the POS API with exponential backoff retry. On success, the POS order ID is stored. On failure after max retries, the order is marked FAILED and flagged for manual review.

steps.title: Real-Time Sync
steps.desc: Every state change is persisted to PostgreSQL and broadcast via WebSocket to all connected dashboard clients  -  no polling, no page refreshes.

images.alt: OrderBridge dashboard showing live order feed
images.caption: Live dashboard  -  real-time order feed with status tracking
Orders page  -  filterable, sortable, CSV export

images.alt: MockPOS mock terminal showing injected orders
images.caption: MockPOS terminal  -  orders arriving automatically from OrderBridge
Simulator  -  fire test orders from any delivery platform

items.name: React 19 + Vite
items.reason: Fast dev experience, React Query for server state, no SSR overhead needed for a dashboard.

items.name: Fastify + TypeScript
items.reason: Lower overhead than Express, schema-based validation built in, excellent TypeScript support.

items.name: PostgreSQL + Prisma
items.reason: Relational data fits the order/token/mapping schema well. Prisma keeps migrations safe and typed.

items.name: WebSockets (ws)
items.reason: Native WebSocket  -  no Socket.io overhead for a single broadcast channel.

items.name: OAuth 2.0 + AES-256
items.reason: Industry-standard POS auth flow. Tokens encrypted at rest before writing to the database.

items.name: HMAC-SHA256
items.reason: Webhook signature verification  -  only legitimate delivery platform payloads are processed.

laomaitravelCaseStudy.tagline: A bilingual EN/TH tourism website built for SEO, web traffic analytics, and booking inquiries  -  no backend required.
laomaitravelCaseStudy.heroImageAlt: Lao Mai Travel hero section  -  temple at dusk with navy and gold design

problem.p1: Before this site, Lao Mai Travel had no idea if their website was working. No data on who visited, where they came from, what they looked at, or what device they used. Every business decision about the site was a guess.
problem.p2: They also had no bilingual routing  -  visitors arriving in Lao had no way to read the site in their own language. And the only way to contact them was a phone number buried at the bottom of the page.
problems: No visibility into who was visiting or what was working
No bilingual support for Lao-speaking visitors
No booking inquiry system  -  just a phone number

design.p1: The brief was premium, local, trustworthy. Not generic travel-site bright colors.
design.p2: Navy and gold  -  navy reads as serious and professional, gold references Lao temple architecture and Buddhist heritage directly. The combination earns trust before a word is read.
design.p3: Typography in three layers: Cinzel for navigation (classical, carved-stone quality), Cormorant Garamond for headlines (high-contrast, luxury editorial feel), Montserrat for body text (clean, readable). Each has a job. None compete.
design.p4: The hero puts a temple photograph full-screen at dusk with the headline split between white roman and gold italic. The site immediately feels like the place it's selling.

nextjs.intro: Three reasons: SEO, bilingual routing, no backend to maintain.

reasons.title: SEO-first rendering
reasons.desc: Every tour and destination page renders as full HTML on the server. Search engines index it all  -  no JavaScript required. For a business where customers search "Laos trekking tours" or "Bolaven Plateau homestay", that's directly tied to bookings.

reasons.title: Bilingual routing that's first-class
reasons.desc: The App Router handles bilingual routing cleanly  -  all pages live under app/[locale]/, so /en and /th are proper first-class routes with their own metadata, not a hack on top of a single-language site. next-intl manages translations.

reasons.title: No backend to maintain
reasons.desc: Resend handles the booking form emails. Vercel handles deployment  -  no server to configure, no maintenance, no downtime to manage. The client's site runs itself.

analytics.heading: From guessing to knowing.
analytics.p1: Umami tracks every visit in real time  -  pages viewed, time on site, country of origin, referrer source (Google, Instagram, direct link), and device type. The client sees all of it in a clean dashboard with no technical knowledge required.
analytics.p2: Umami is also privacy-first  -  no cookies, no personal data stored, GDPR compliant out of the box. No consent banner cluttering the site. For a business serving international tourists from Europe and beyond, that's not just ethical  -  it avoids a legal headache.
analytics.p3: The client accesses their dashboard through a single shared link. No account, no password, no monthly subscription. They check it like they check their WhatsApp  -  independently, on their own time, without calling me.
questions: The Bolaven Plateau tour page gets 600 views a month but almost no inquiries  -  is the pricing too high, or is the page not converting?
65% of visitors are on mobile  -  is the booking form easy enough to fill out on a phone?
Traffic spikes every November and December  -  that's when to push promotions.
Traffic is coming from Australia and France but almost nothing from the US  -  where should social media efforts focus?

images.alt: Umami analytics main dashboard showing visits and page views
images.caption: Main dashboard  -  real-time visits, bounce rate, and session duration

images.alt: Umami pages and traffic sources breakdown
images.caption: Pages & sources  -  which tours get traffic and where it comes from
Visitor locations  -  knowing where to focus marketing efforts
Destinations  -  off-the-beaten-path locations across Laos

images.alt: Lao Mai Travel tour packages page
images.caption: Tour packages  -  highland treks, Mekong islands, cultural homestays

images.alt: Lao Mai Travel booking inquiry form
images.caption: Booking form  -  powered by Resend, delivered straight to the client

images.alt: Thai language version of the site
images.caption: Thai language routing  -  full /th route with its own metadata

items.name: Next.js 15 + App Router
items.reason: Server-rendered pages for SEO, locale-first routing via app/[locale]/, and edge-ready deployment on Vercel.

items.name: TypeScript
items.reason: Type-safe development across the full codebase  -  catches errors before they reach production.

items.name: CSS Modules
items.reason: Scoped styles without runtime overhead. Ideal for a design-heavy site with layered typography and a strong visual identity.

items.name: next-intl
items.reason: First-class bilingual routing with per-locale metadata, full EN/TH translation coverage, and no locale hacks.

items.name: Resend
items.reason: Handles booking inquiry emails via a clean API  -  no backend server needed, reliable delivery.

items.name: Umami
items.reason: Web Traffic Analytics  -  no cookies, GDPR compliant, self-hostable. Client accesses their dashboard via a single shared link.

items.name: Vercel
items.reason: Zero-config deployment with automatic previews, instant rollbacks, and no infrastructure to manage.

pmlaosCaseStudy.tagline: A multi-language EN/LO/ZH real estate platform.  Installable as an App (PWA), Google OAuth-secured admin, and server-rendered property pages built for a business in Vientiane.
pmlaosCaseStudy.heroImageAlt: PM Real Estate Laos homepage  -  property listings with clean professional layout

outcomes.label: Three languages, or as many as needed
Easily login with your Google Account
You can install this website like an app

problem.p1: Before this platform, PM Real Estate Laos managed their listings across social media posts. There was no central place where buyers could browse all available properties, filter by area, or view high-quality photos  -  and every update meant editing a file and resending it manually.
problem.p2: The agency also had no system for tracking clients or deals. Pipeline status lived in spreadsheets and WhatsApp (BAD IDEA), follow-ups were missed, and there was no way to see at a glance which properties were active, pending, or sold.
problems: No central listing platform  -  properties scattered across Facebook and WhatsApp
No client or deal pipeline  -  everything tracked in disconnected spreadsheets
No multilingual support  -  missing the Chinese-speaking and international buyer segments entirely

design.p1: The brief was professional, trustworthy, and built for an international audience. Real estate buyers  -  especially foreign investors  -  make decisions based on perceived legitimacy before they ever see a property.
design.p2: Clean layouts with large property photography at the centre. The UI stays out of the way and lets the listings do the work. Consistent typographic hierarchy ensures property details are scannable at a glance.
design.p3: The admin dashboard mirrors the public-facing design language  -  dark, minimal, data-dense. Using the same visual system for both sides means the client feels at home in the CMS from day one.

nextjs.intro: Three reasons: SEO for individual listings, trilingual routing, and a backend API without a separate server.

reasons.title: SEO-first listing pages
reasons.desc: Every property page is server-rendered with full metadata, structured data, and Open Graph tags. When buyers search 'apartment for rent Vientiane' or 'land for sale Laos', individual listing pages show up  -  not just the homepage.

reasons.title: Multi-language routing
reasons.desc: All pages live under app/[locale]/ with EN, LO, and ZH as proper first-class routes. next-intl handles translations and locale detection. No redirect hacks, no duplicated templates.

reasons.title: Admin API in the same codebase
reasons.desc: Next.js Route Handlers power the entire CMS backend. No separate Express server to deploy, no CORS to configure. The admin dashboard is part of the same deployment as the public site.

images.alt: PM Real Estate Laos homepage with property listings
images.caption: Homepage  -  property listings with search and filter

images.alt: Multiple property listings in gallery view
images.caption: Listings gallery  -  grid view with property thumbnails
Listing detail page  -  photos, specs, and contact form

admin.heading: A full CMS, built in
admin.p1: The agency needed more than a website  -  they needed a system. The admin dashboard is a full property management tool: add and edit listings, manage clients, track deal pipeline, and control which areas appear on the site.
admin.p2: Authentication is handled via username and password and by Google OAuth. The team can sign-in with their existing Google accounts.

images.caption: Main dashboard
Track every property transaction
Client management  -  buyer and seller database
Area management  -  control which zones appear on the site
Google OAuth login
Mobile-responsive admin  -  manage listings from anywhere

items.name: Next.js 15 + TypeScript
items.reason: Server-rendered listing pages with App Router. TypeScript across the entire codebase  -  no runtime surprises in the admin or the API.

items.name: next-intl
items.reason: Trilingual EN/LO/ZH routing baked in from day one. Each locale is a first-class route with its own metadata and translated content.

items.name: Prisma + PostgreSQL
items.reason: Relational schema for properties, clients, deals, and areas. Prisma keeps migrations safe and typed  -  no raw SQL in the codebase.

items.name: Google OAuth
items.reason: The client uses their existing Google account or username and password.

items.name: CSS Modules
items.reason: Scoped styles per component. No Tailwind

items.name: PWA + Service Worker
items.reason: The site installs to the homescreen like a native app. Property agents in the field open it without a browser  -  and cached listings load even on a weak signal.

cta.heading: Need a platform like this?
cta.subtitle: I build web platforms for Vientiane businesses  -  property, tourism, services. Full-stack, multilingual, and built to be managed by the client, not a developer.

vientianeWebsiteArticle.breadcrumbCurrent: Why Vientiane Businesses Need a Website
vientianeWebsiteArticle.lede: If you own a business in Vientiane and you only have a Facebook page, you are losing money. A lot of it.
vientianeWebsiteArticle.figHero: PM Laos appearing on Google - a real Vientiane business with a proper online presence

google.label: [ GOOGLE vs FACEBOOK ]
google.heading: Google First, Facebook Later
google.p1: The way people search for businesses in the world goes like this: when someone needs a service, a restaurant, a hotel, or a supplier, they don't scroll endlessly through Facebook. They go to Google.
google.p2: Think about your own behavior. When you need something specific - "best coffee in Vientiane", "apartment for rent Vientiane", "web developer Laos" - you search on Google. Your customers do the same.
google.p3: Facebook is where people go to socialize. Google is where people go with intention: ready to buy, ready to contact, ready to decide, ready to choose.
google.p4: If your business only exists on Facebook, you are missing those high-intent customers.

identity.label: [ THE IDENTITY PROBLEM ]
identity.heading: Too Many Facebook Pages, No Clear Identity
identity.p1: Try searching for a business on Facebook. You'll often find:
identity.p3: Now compare that with a website. Your domain name (like PMLaos.com) is unique. No duplicates. No confusion. One official source of truth.

vientianeWebsiteArticle.figCopycats: Facebook copycat pages - one of the most common problems businesses face without a real website

ownership.label: [ YOU DON'T OWN IT ]
ownership.heading: You Don't Own Facebook
ownership.p1: This is the uncomfortable truth: your Facebook page is not really yours. At any moment:
risks: Your reach can drop to near zero
ownership.p2: All it takes is one mistake, one misunderstanding, or even a few reports. Someone hacked your Facebook and you lost it forever.
ownership.p3: A website is different. You own it. You control it. No algorithm decides if your customers can see your business.
ownership.p4: When that happens with Facebook, your entire online presence can disappear overnight.

vientianeWebsiteArticle.figUrl: PMLaos.com - a unique domain that can't be duplicated, reported, or taken away

friction.label: [ STOP THE FRICTION ]
friction.heading: Stop Making Customers Work to Find Information
friction.p1: On Facebook, information is scattered across posts and pictures, which is really annoying. A customer might have to:
painPoints: Still not find what they need
friction.p2: Most people won't do that. They'll leave and go to a competitor.
friction.p3: A website organizes everything clearly: services, prices, contact details, location, and FAQs - all in one place, easy to access.

vientianeWebsiteArticle.figListing: PM Laos property listing - all information organized, clear, and accessible without any scrolling

professionalism.label: [ PROFESSIONALISM ]
professionalism.heading: Your Business Looks More Professional
professionalism.p1: Perception matters. A business with a clean, modern website looks more established, more trustworthy, and more serious.
professionalism.p2: A business with only a Facebook page often looks small, temporary, or less reliable - even if that is not true. And in competitive markets, that perception alone can decide who gets the customer.
professionalism.calloutText: Anyone can create a Facebook page. Not everyone invests in a real website.

vientianeWebsiteArticle.figHomepage: PM Laos website homepage - a clean, professional online presence that builds instant credibility

storefront.label: [ YOUR DIGITAL STOREFRONT ]
storefront.heading: Your Website Is Your Digital Storefront
storefront.p1: Imagine your website as the digital home of your business. Just like a physical shop welcomes customers, your website is where people discover your services, understand your value, and decide to contact you. It becomes the center of everything you do online.

benefits.title: Your Central Hub
benefits.desc: All traffic - from Google, Facebook, TikTok, or email - leads back to your website.

benefits.title: Credibility & Trust
benefits.desc: Showcase testimonials, portfolio, and case studies to turn visitors into customers faster.

benefits.title: 24/7 Availability
benefits.desc: Customers can learn about your business, contact you, or book services at any time.

benefits.title: Cost-Effective Marketing
benefits.desc: SEO brings free, organic traffic from Google - often better customers than paid ads.

benefits.title: Targeted Messaging
benefits.desc: Speak directly to your ideal customer with pages tailored to specific services and needs.

benefits.title: Email Marketing
benefits.desc: Capture leads and turn visitors into email subscribers - and later into paying customers.

benefits.title: Data & Insights
benefits.desc: Understand who visits your site, what they look for, and how they found you.

reality.label: [ THE REALITY ]
reality.heading: The Businesses That Win
reality.p1: I've been building websites and digital systems for businesses around the world since 2015. The pattern is always the same: businesses with a strong website grow faster, attract better customers, and build stronger brands.
reality.p2: No website? You are losing customers. Not because your business is bad - but because people can't find you, don't trust what they see, or give up before contacting you.
reality.p3: A website is no longer optional. It's the foundation of your online presence.
reality.p4: In a growing city like Vientiane, where more people rely on the internet every day to make decisions, the businesses that invest in a proper digital presence will win. The rest will slowly fall behind.
reality.calloutText: If you're ready to build a website that actually brings you customers, that's exactly what I do.
reality.p5: Not sure? Ask ChatGPT: "What opportunities does a business miss in Vientiane if it doesn't have a website?" The answer might surprise you.
